title: Unit 4 HCIIIA — Mastery Bank
description: BPH/TURP, Prazosin, Finasteride, Appendicitis, Peritonitis, TPN/Enteral, GI conditions. All 6 NGN formats, all 6 layers.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient post-TURP with continuous bladder irrigation (CBI). The drainage bag reads 3,600 mL. The nurse has infused 3,100 mL of irrigation solution. What is the patient's TRUE urine output?
questions.rationale: TRUE URINE OUTPUT = Total Drainage − Irrigation Infused. 3,600 − 3,100 = 500 mL. If you fail to subtract the irrigation, you may falsely assume adequate urine output when the patient may actually be in renal failure. This formula is critical post-TURP.

option_rationales.A: 3,600 mL is the total drainage bag volume — it includes both irrigation and true urine. This is incorrect.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. 3,600 − 3,100 = 500 mL. This is the true urine output after subtracting irrigation infused.
option_rationales.C: 3,100 mL is the amount of irrigation infused, not urine output.
option_rationales.D: 6,700 mL is the sum of both volumes — a meaningless figure that represents no clinical measurement.

questions.text: When should the first dose of Prazosin (Minipress) be administered to a patient with BPH?

options.C: Two hours before the first void of the day
options.D: With the largest meal of the day

questions.rationale: Prazosin causes significant vasodilation via alpha-1 blockade, which leads to a sudden drop in blood pressure (orthostatic hypotension) after the first dose — a phenomenon called 'first-dose syncope.' Administering at bedtime allows the patient to sleep through the peak hypotensive effect, preventing falls.

option_rationales.A: Morning administration would expose the patient to peak hypotensive effects while awake and mobile — high fall risk.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. First dose at bedtime allows the patient to sleep through peak vasodilation, preventing syncope and falls.
option_rationales.C: This timing has no pharmacological basis and still carries syncope risk.
option_rationales.D: Meal timing does not mitigate the orthostatic hypotension risk of the first dose.

questions.text: Which instruction is MOST critical when teaching a female patient of childbearing potential who handles medications in her household about Finasteride (Proscar)?

options.A: Take this medication with food to reduce nausea
options.C: Never crush, break, or handle the tablets without gloves if pregnant or may become pregnant
options.D: Monitor blood pressure daily while the patient takes this drug

questions.rationale: Finasteride is a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor that blocks testosterone-to-DHT conversion. It is absorbed through the skin. Pregnant individuals who handle crushed or broken tablets absorb the drug, which crosses the placenta and causes abnormal development of male fetal genitalia. Film-coated tablets that are intact are safer — they must NEVER be crushed.

option_rationales.A: Food timing is not the critical teaching for Finasteride — safety of handling is the priority.
option_rationales.B: Finasteride requires 3–6 months to show effect, not 1–2 weeks. This is also incorrect content.
option_rationales.C: CORRECT. Dermal absorption of Finasteride by pregnant individuals causes fetal harm. This is the most critical and life-altering safety teaching.
option_rationales.D: Finasteride does not significantly affect blood pressure — that is Prazosin. This reflects the classic interference pair confusion.

questions.text: Which sequence of symptoms is CHARACTERISTIC of appendicitis and helps distinguish it from gastroenteritis?

options.A: Nausea and vomiting → then right lower quadrant pain
options.B: Diarrhea → then fever → then abdominal pain
options.C: Right lower quadrant pain → then nausea and vomiting
options.D: Fever → then constipation → then right lower quadrant pain

questions.rationale: In appendicitis, PAIN comes FIRST — typically starting periumbilically and migrating to the right lower quadrant (McBurney's point), followed by nausea and vomiting. In gastroenteritis, GI symptoms (nausea/vomiting/diarrhea) come first. This sequence is a high-yield differentiating fact tested on NCLEX.

option_rationales.A: N/V before pain is the gastroenteritis pattern, not appendicitis.
option_rationales.B: Diarrhea as the first symptom suggests gastroenteritis or IBD, not appendicitis.
option_rationales.C: CORRECT. Pain FIRST (RLQ, McBurney's point), THEN nausea/vomiting. This is the diagnostic sequence for appendicitis.
option_rationales.D: Fever and constipation first is not the classic appendicitis presentation.

questions.text: A nurse reviews the intake and output record for a patient 6 hours post-TURP with continuous bladder irrigation (CBI). The drainage bag contains 4,800 mL of fluid. The irrigation solution hung has delivered 4,800 mL since the catheter was placed. Which interpretation is CORRECT?

options.A: The patient has an excellent urine output of 800 mL/hr
options.B: The patient's true urine output is 0 mL and warrants immediate provider notification
options.C: The drainage bag needs to be emptied since it equals the input
options.D: The CBI rate should be increased because output equals input

questions.rationale: TRUE URINE = Total Drainage − Irrigation Infused. 4,800 − 4,800 = 0 mL. A true urine output of 0 mL six hours post-op suggests acute urinary retention or renal compromise — a critical finding requiring immediate provider notification. Failing to subtract irrigation volume would falsely suggest adequate output.

option_rationales.A: This interprets the drainage bag volume as urine — a critical error. 4,800 mL is ALL drainage, most of which is irrigation.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. 4,800 − 4,800 = 0 mL true urine. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate notification.
option_rationales.C: Emptying the bag is a nursing action but not the correct interpretation of this data.
option_rationales.D: Increasing CBI rate is not the correct response when true urine output is zero.

questions.text: A patient started Prazosin 3 days ago. Vital signs are: supine BP 128/82, sitting BP 112/74, standing BP 104/70. The patient reports feeling dizzy when standing. What should the nurse document?

options.A: Normal blood pressure response; no intervention required
options.B: Orthostatic hypotension — systolic drop of 24 mmHg and diastolic drop of 12 mmHg on standing
options.C: Hypertensive urgency requiring immediate provider notification
options.D: Hypoglycemia — glucose check required before reporting

questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a systolic drop ≥20 mmHg OR diastolic drop ≥10 mmHg when moving from lying to standing. This patient shows a 24 mmHg systolic drop and 12 mmHg diastolic drop — both criteria are met. This is a known adverse effect of Prazosin (alpha-1 blockade causes vasodilation). Teaching: rise slowly, avoid hot showers and alcohol.

option_rationales.A: A 24 mmHg systolic drop exceeds the ≥20 mmHg threshold — this is clinically significant orthostatic hypotension.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. Systolic drop 24 mmHg (≥20 threshold met), diastolic drop 12 mmHg (≥10 threshold met). Classic Prazosin adverse effect.
option_rationales.C: These values indicate hypotension, not hypertension. Hypertensive urgency is BP >180/120.
option_rationales.D: Dizziness from Prazosin is vascular, not glycemic. Glucose monitoring is not the priority here.

questions.text: A nursing student asks why Finasteride (Proscar) takes 3–6 months to relieve BPH symptoms while Prazosin provides relief within hours. Which explanation is CORRECT?

options.A: Finasteride is absorbed more slowly because it is enteric-coated
options.B: Finasteride must inhibit testosterone-to-DHT conversion long enough to allow the prostate to physically shrink, which requires months
options.C: Finasteride works by relaxing smooth muscle, which requires time to accumulate in tissue
options.D: Prazosin works faster because it has a higher bioavailability

questions.rationale: Prazosin is an alpha-1 blocker — it relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck within hours, providing immediate symptom relief (but the prostate size is unchanged). Finasteride is a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor — it blocks testosterone→DHT conversion. DHT is the hormone that drives prostate cell growth. Without DHT, the prostate slowly involutes (shrinks) over 3–6 months. These two drugs are often prescribed together: Prazosin for immediate relief, Finasteride for long-term size reduction.

option_rationales.A: The coating type does not explain the mechanism. Finasteride is film-coated to prevent handling risk, not for delayed release.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. Finasteride blocks testosterone→DHT conversion. Without DHT, prostate cells stop proliferating and the gland shrinks — a physical process that takes 3–6 months.
option_rationales.C: Relaxing smooth muscle is Prazosin's mechanism, not Finasteride's.
option_rationales.D: Bioavailability differences do not explain the mechanism difference — this answer misattributes cause.

questions.text: A patient is admitted with appendicitis. The nurse knows that a WBC of 22,000/mm³ (compared to the normal 10,000–18,000 range for appendicitis) is most concerning for which complication?

options.B: Perforation of the appendix with peritonitis
options.C: Septicemia from a urinary tract infection
options.D: Appendiceal abscess formation requiring drainage only

questions.rationale: In uncomplicated appendicitis, WBC is typically 10,000–18,000/mm³. A WBC >20,000/mm³ strongly suggests perforation has already occurred, which introduces bowel bacteria into the peritoneal cavity and causes peritonitis — a life-threatening emergency. Perforation can occur within 24 hours of symptom onset, hence why same-day appendectomy is standard of care.

option_rationales.A: Dehydration does not cause WBC elevation of this magnitude.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. WBC >20,000 is the threshold for suspected perforation → peritonitis. This requires emergency surgical intervention.
option_rationales.C: A UTI could elevate WBC but would have urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency) and would not explain RLQ tenderness/guarding.
option_rationales.D: An abscess alone does not typically drive WBC this high in isolation, and 'drainage only' understates the urgency.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient 2 hours after TURP with CBI. Which nursing assessments and actions are APPROPRIATE at this time? Select ALL that apply.

options.A: Calculate true urine output by subtracting irrigation infused from total drainage
options.B: Reassure the patient that urge to void with the catheter in place is abnormal and should be reported
options.C: Assess CBI drainage color — blood-tinged to pink is expected; bright red with clots warrants immediate notification
options.D: Position the drainage bag above the level of the bladder to promote drainage
options.E: Instruct the patient to drink 2,500 mL of fluid per day unless contraindicated
options.F: Empty the drainage bag when it is two-thirds full

questions.rationale: Post-TURP CBI care requires: (A) accurate output calculation using the formula; (C) color assessment — normal is blood-tinged to pink, clearing by discharge; bright red with clots requires immediate notification; (E) 2,500 mL/day to prevent catheter clotting; (F) empty at 2/3 full to prevent backflow. B is incorrect — urge to void WITH a catheter in place is NORMAL and expected (large catheter stimulates the sensation). D is incorrect — the drainage bag must be BELOW the bladder at all times to prevent backflow and infection.

option_rationales.A: CORRECT. TRUE URINE = Total − Irrigation. Always required for accurate output documentation.
option_rationales.B: INCORRECT. Urge to void with catheter IN PLACE is NORMAL. The large-bore catheter (20–24 French) stimulates bladder contraction sensation. Telling patients this is abnormal causes unnecessary anxiety.
option_rationales.C: CORRECT. Expected color progression: blood-tinged → pink → clear by discharge. Bright red + clots = active bleeding requiring immediate provider notification.
option_rationales.D: INCORRECT. Drainage bag MUST be BELOW bladder level. Raising it allows urine to flow backward into the bladder, increasing infection and obstruction risk.
option_rationales.E: CORRECT. 2,500 mL/day fluid intake helps keep the catheter patent and prevents clot formation in the bladder.
option_rationales.F: CORRECT. Emptying at 2/3 full prevents the bag from becoming too heavy (risk of disconnection) and prevents backflow.

questions.text: A nurse assesses a patient 12 hours post-appendectomy. Which findings indicate that the patient may be developing peritonitis and should be reported IMMEDIATELY? Select ALL that apply.

options.A: Abdomen is soft and non-tender on palpation
options.D: Bowel sounds audible in all four quadrants
options.G: Patient reporting feeling better and requesting a meal

questions.rationale: Classic peritonitis signs: rigid/board-like abdomen (involuntary guarding from peritoneal inflammation), fever >38.3°C, WBC >20,000 suggesting infection/perforation, and tachycardia from inflammatory/septic response. A soft abdomen, present bowel sounds, and feeling better are reassuring findings inconsistent with peritonitis.

option_rationales.A: Soft, non-tender abdomen is a REASSURING finding — inconsistent with peritonitis.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. Rigid, board-like abdomen is the CLASSIC sign of peritonitis — involuntary abdominal muscle guarding from peritoneal irritation.
option_rationales.C: CORRECT. Temperature ≥38.3°C (101°F) signals active infection/inflammation. This must be reported immediately.
option_rationales.D: Audible bowel sounds suggest the bowel is still motile — reassuring finding. Peritonitis causes absent/diminished bowel sounds (ileus).
option_rationales.E: CORRECT. WBC >20,000/mm³ suggests perforation has occurred or active peritonitis is developing.
option_rationales.F: CORRECT. Tachycardia (HR 118) in this context indicates systemic inflammatory response — an early sepsis sign.
option_rationales.G: Feeling better and requesting meals is a reassuring sign inconsistent with the acute distress of peritonitis.

questions.text: A nurse is comparing two BPH medications. Medication A works within hours, lowers blood pressure, and is given at bedtime. Medication B requires 3–6 months to work, lowers PSA by ~50%, and must never be handled crushed by pregnant individuals. Which pairing is CORRECT?

options.A: Medication A = Finasteride; Medication B = Prazosin
options.B: Medication A = Prazosin; Medication B = Finasteride

questions.rationale: Medication A is Prazosin (alpha-1 blocker): rapid onset hours, lowers BP via vasodilation, given at bedtime to prevent first-dose syncope. Medication B is Finasteride (5-alpha-reductase inhibitor): slow onset 3–6 months, shrinks prostate by blocking DHT, lowers PSA by ~50%, and cannot be handled crushed by pregnant individuals due to teratogenicity.

option_rationales.A: INCORRECT. This has the two medications reversed — classic interference pair confusion.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. Prazosin = fast, BP-lowering, bedtime. Finasteride = slow, prostate-shrinking, teratogenic when crushed.
option_rationales.C: Finasteride is NOT an alpha-1 blocker — it is a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor. This is the key mechanism distinction.
option_rationales.D: Finasteride requires 3–6 months. Only Prazosin acts within hours.

questions.text: A nurse is distinguishing between TPN and enteral tube feeding. Which statement is CORRECT?

options.A: TPN uses the GI tract and has a lower infection risk than enteral feeding
options.B: Enteral feeding requires a central line and must be tapered to prevent hypoglycemia
options.C: TPN must be tapered off gradually; abrupt discontinuation causes hypoglycemia
options.D: Enteral feeding tubing is changed every 24 hours

questions.rationale: TPN (Total Parenteral Nutrition) delivers concentrated dextrose intravenously, causing the pancreas to produce high levels of insulin. Abrupt discontinuation leaves insulin circulating without glucose input → hypoglycemia. If TPN is unavailable, D10W is infused to bridge. Enteral feeding uses the GI tract (not IV) and does NOT require tapering. Enteral has lower infection risk; TPN uses a central line with higher infection risk. TPN tubing changes every 24 hours (not enteral).

option_rationales.A: INCORRECT. TPN bypasses the GI tract entirely and uses a central line. It has HIGHER infection risk, not lower.
option_rationales.B: INCORRECT. Enteral feeding uses a tube into the GI tract, not a central line. Tapering is required for TPN, not enteral.
option_rationales.C: CORRECT. TPN causes prolonged high insulin secretion. Abrupt stop → hypoglycemia. Bridge with D10W if TPN unavailable.
option_rationales.D: Enteral feeding tubing is changed per protocol (not every 24 hours). TPN tubing changes every 24 hours — this is a classic reversal error.

questions.case_study: A 68-year-old male is 4 hours post-TURP. His CBI is running. The drainage bag has collected 1,800 mL; 1,200 mL of irrigation has been infused. The patient is restless and reports suprapubic pressure. Vital signs: BP 148/90, HR 102, Temp 37.2°C. The CBI outflow tubing appears kinked.
questions.text: Based on the scenario above, complete the Bowtie by identifying the condition the patient is most likely experiencing, the 2 priority actions to take, and the 2 parameters to monitor.

actions_to_take: Unkink and irrigate the outflow tubing
Increase the CBI infusion rate to 300 mL/hr
parameters_to_monitor: CBI drainage color and clot presence

actions: Unkink and irrigate the outflow tubing
parameters: CBI drainage color and clot presence

questions.rationale: The kinked tubing is the most likely cause of the restlessness and suprapubic pressure — obstruction prevents drainage, causing bladder distension. True urine output is 600 mL (1,800 − 1,200), which appears adequate; however, the obstruction must be resolved immediately. Notify the provider when obstruction does not resolve easily. Key parameters post-TURP: drainage color (blood-tinged → pink → clear is normal; bright red with clots = abnormal) and true urine output.

bowtie_rationales.condition: Kinked outflow tubing prevents CBI drainage → urine and irrigation accumulate → bladder distension → suprapubic pressure and restlessness. Tachycardia is from discomfort, not sepsis (no fever). Vital signs do not yet indicate shock.
bowtie_rationales.actions: First: resolve the mechanical obstruction (unkink/irrigate tubing). Second: notify provider — obstruction post-TURP is a clinical priority and may require gentle manual irrigation or catheter replacement if tubing correction alone fails.
bowtie_rationales.parameters: Drainage color tells you if bleeding is worsening (bright red = emergent). True urine output (drainage − irrigation) is the gold-standard post-TURP renal function indicator.

questions.case_study: A nurse is reviewing the assessment findings for a patient 8 hours post-TURP with CBI. The following data is collected.
questions.text: For each assessment finding, indicate whether it is Expected (normal post-TURP) or Unexpected (requires provider notification).

rows.label: Patient states urge to void with catheter in place
Drainage bag at waist level (same height as patient's hip)
True urine output 75 mL for past hour

questions.rationale: r1: Pink/blood-tinged drainage is expected at 8 hours post-TURP, clearing by discharge. r2: The sensation of needing to void WITH the catheter in place is NORMAL — the large-bore catheter (20–24 Fr) irritates the bladder trigone, causing a false urge. r3: The drainage bag must ALWAYS be below the level of the bladder. Waist height is at or above bladder level — this risks backflow and infection. r4: Temperature >38.3°C (101°F) suggests infection/sepsis — must be reported immediately. r5: 75 mL/hr true urine output is within acceptable range (goal ≥30 mL/hr).

cell_rationales.r1: Blood-tinged to pink drainage is the expected color 8 hours post-TURP. Bright red with clots would be unexpected.
cell_rationales.r2: The urge to void with a catheter is NORMAL post-TURP. The 20–24 French catheter stimulates the bladder neck sensation. Teaching this reduces patient anxiety.
cell_rationales.r3: The drainage bag MUST be below bladder level at all times to prevent backflow. Waist height is level with or above the bladder — this is an error requiring correction.
cell_rationales.r4: Fever >38.3°C (101°F) post-TURP indicates possible urinary tract infection or urosepsis — provider must be notified immediately.
cell_rationales.r5: 75 mL/hr true urine output (after subtracting irrigation) is adequate. The minimum acceptable is 30 mL/hr.

questions.case_study: A 22-year-old patient admitted with right lower quadrant pain is being monitored over 12 hours. The nurse reviews the following assessment data.
questions.text: Review the trend data below. For each parameter, select the trend over the 12-hour period.
questions.rationale: All four parameters are trending in a dangerous direction: WBC rising from 13,500 → 23,400 (>20,000 = perforation risk), pain escalating (5 → 9), temperature rising toward 102.4°F (fever), and systolic BP dropping (124 → 98 mmHg, approaching hypotension). This pattern is consistent with appendiceal perforation progressing toward peritonitis and septic shock. The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY and prepare for emergency surgery.

questions.text: Complete the nursing teaching note for a patient newly prescribed both BPH medications.

sentence_parts: The nurse teaches the patient: Prazosin should be taken at
to shrink the prostate, and pregnant individuals must NEVER handle
due to risk of fetal harm.

questions.rationale: Prazosin: first dose at BEDTIME to sleep through first-dose syncope risk (alpha-1 vasodilation drops BP acutely). Finasteride: 3–6 months to work (shrinks prostate via DHT blockade). Pregnant individuals must NOT handle CRUSHED tablets — the film coating on intact tablets provides a protective barrier, but crushing removes it and allows dermal absorption → fetal teratogenicity.

questions.text: A patient is admitted with suspected appendicitis. The nurse prepares the patient for emergency appendectomy. Which nursing actions are APPROPRIATE at this time? Select ALL that apply.

options.B: Apply a heating pad to the right lower quadrant to reduce pain
options.C: Administer a Fleet enema to relieve constipation
options.D: Establish IV access and begin IV fluid replacement
options.E: Position the patient in semi-Fowler's for comfort
options.F: Monitor WBC and notify provider if greater than 20,000

questions.rationale: Appropriate pre-op appendicitis care: NPO for surgical preparation; IV access and fluids to maintain perfusion; semi-Fowler's for comfort and to facilitate drainage if rupture occurs; WBC monitoring for perforation risk. B (heating pad) is CONTRAINDICATED — heat increases inflammation and can precipitate rupture. C (enema) is CONTRAINDICATED — increases intraluminal pressure and can cause perforation.
questions.source_reference: Unit4_MASTERY_Guide.html (Cluster B — 'Don't Rupture It' Protocol); Prof-Unit-4-HCIIIA-2026.pdf

option_rationales.A: CORRECT. NPO is required for surgical preparation — oral intake must stop immediately.
option_rationales.B: INCORRECT. Heat increases inflammation and blood flow to the area — this can accelerate perforation. NEVER apply heat to a suspected inflamed appendix.
option_rationales.C: INCORRECT. Enemas increase intraluminal pressure in the colon, which can rupture an inflamed, distended appendix. This is explicitly contraindicated.
option_rationales.D: CORRECT. IV access is essential for fluid replacement, antibiotic administration, and anesthesia induction.
option_rationales.E: CORRECT. Semi-Fowler's promotes comfort (reduces abdominal tension), promotes lung expansion, and helps localize any leaked fluid to the pelvis if rupture occurs.
option_rationales.F: CORRECT. WBC >20,000/mm³ is the clinical threshold suggesting perforation has occurred — provider must be notified immediately.

questions.text: A patient on Finasteride for 6 months has a PSA result of 2.4 ng/mL. His provider says "we need to double this value to interpret it correctly." The patient asks the nurse why. What is the BEST explanation?

options.A: Finasteride causes PSA to be released more slowly into the blood
options.B: Finasteride inhibits DHT, which lowers PSA production by about 50%; the true PSA is estimated by doubling the result
options.C: The lab machine is calibrated differently for patients on Finasteride
options.D: Finasteride increases renal excretion of PSA, making levels appear falsely low

questions.rationale: Finasteride suppresses the prostate's production of PSA by approximately 50% as a secondary effect of DHT suppression. If a patient's true PSA is 4.8 ng/mL but they are on Finasteride, the measured result will be ~2.4 ng/mL. Clinicians must DOUBLE the measured value to estimate the true PSA for prostate cancer screening purposes.

option_rationales.A: PSA release rate is not the mechanism — it's about suppressed production.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. Finasteride lowers PSA by ~50% through DHT suppression. The lab value must be doubled for accurate prostate cancer screening interpretation.
option_rationales.C: The lab machine is not recalibrated — the clinician adjusts for the drug effect manually.
option_rationales.D: PSA is not renally cleared in a way that Finasteride would affect. This is a fabricated mechanism.

questions.case_study: A nurse is caring for two patients: Patient A has TPN running via a central line. Patient B has enteral tube feeding via a nasogastric tube. The nurse prepares to manage both patients' nutritional support.
questions.text: For each nursing action, indicate whether it applies to TPN, Enteral Feeding, or Both.

rows.label: Confirm tube placement via x-ray before first feeding
Taper off gradually to prevent hypoglycemia

questions.rationale: TPN uses concentrated dextrose IV → hyperglycemia risk → glucose monitoring. Enteral: confirm placement by x-ray before starting. TPN tubing every 24 hours per policy; enteral per protocol. TPN must be tapered (abrupt stop → hypoglycemia from residual insulin); enteral can be stopped without tapering. Enteral is held for medication administration; TPN is IV and not affected by oral/tube meds.

cell_rationales.r1: TPN delivers concentrated dextrose IV — the pancreas secretes high insulin, raising hyperglycemia risk. Blood glucose monitoring every 4–6 hours is standard TPN care.
cell_rationales.r2: Enteral feeding via NG tube MUST have placement confirmed by x-ray before the first feeding. Misplacement into the airway causes fatal aspiration if feeding is started.
cell_rationales.r3: TPN tubing changes every 24 hours due to high infection risk (central line + concentrated dextrose is an excellent bacterial growth medium). Enteral tubing follows a different protocol.
cell_rationales.r4: TPN must be tapered — abrupt discontinuation leaves circulating insulin without glucose substrate → hypoglycemia. If TPN is unavailable, bridge with D10W.
cell_rationales.r5: Enteral tube feedings are stopped during medication administration to prevent drug-nutrient interactions (many drugs are absorbed differently with food).

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient with peritonitis. The patient is in severe pain. Analgesics have been ordered. Which action is the nurse's PRIORITY before administering the analgesic?

options.A: Administer the analgesic immediately to reduce pain and promote comfort
options.B: Obtain vital signs and perform a complete abdominal assessment first; analgesics may mask signs of deterioration during the initial workup
options.C: Apply a warm compress to the abdomen to reduce discomfort before giving the medication
options.D: Place the patient supine to facilitate drug absorption

questions.rationale: During the initial workup of peritonitis, analgesics are sometimes withheld because masking pain can obscure the progression of signs (guarding, rebound tenderness, rigidity). The nurse must complete a thorough assessment first, document baseline findings, and notify the provider before administering pain medication. This is a nuanced priority decision — not that pain doesn't matter, but that clinical data collection takes precedence during workup.
questions.source_reference: Unit4_MASTERY_Guide.html (Bowel Obstruction Nursing Interventions — analgesic note); Unit4_MASTERY_Guide.html (Peritonitis section)

option_rationales.A: Comfort matters, but during active workup, analgesics can mask deteriorating peritonitis signs — assessment is the priority.
option_rationales.B: CORRECT. Perform complete assessment first. Pain medication given without baseline data may obscure critical findings (rebound tenderness, guarding rigidity) needed for surgical decision-making.
option_rationales.C: Warm compresses are CONTRAINDICATED with abdominal inflammatory processes — heat increases inflammation and perforation risk.
option_rationales.D: Supine position is not recommended for peritonitis — semi-Fowler's is preferred (pools fluid in pelvis, reduces diaphragm pressure).
